content:
# Exodus Chapter 30 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~51r–51v (PDF pp. 100–101); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 73–74 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_030_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_030_page_002.jpg`, Vaticanus: `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_030_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_030_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Altar of Incense — Where Prayer Rises (vv. 1–10)

**1** "Make an altar for burning incense. Build it from acacia wood. **2** It is to be square — a cubit long and a cubit wide — and two cubits tall. Its horns are to be of one piece with it. **3** Overlay it with pure gold — its top, all its sides, and its horns — and make a gold molding around it. **4** Make two gold rings for it below the molding, on two of its opposite sides, to hold the poles used to carry it. **5** Make the poles from acacia wood and overlay them with gold. **6** Place the altar in front of the curtain that hangs before the ark of the covenant law — before the atonement cover that is over the covenant law — where I will meet with you.

**7** "Aaron is to burn fragrant incense on it every morning when he tends the lamps. **8** And when Aaron lights the lamps at twilight, he is to burn incense again, so that incense rises before YHWH regularly, throughout your generations. **9** Do not offer on this altar any unauthorized incense, or any burnt offering, or any grain offering; and do not pour a drink offering on it. **10** Once a year Aaron is to make atonement on its horns. With the blood of the annual purification offering, he is to make atonement on it once a year throughout your generations. It is most holy to YHWH."

---

### The Ransom Money — Every Life Valued Equally (vv. 11–16)

**11** Then YHWH spoke to Moses: **12** "When you take a census of the Sons of Israel to count them, each man is to pay YHWH a ransom for his life at the time he is counted, so that no plague will strike them when they are numbered. **13** Everyone who crosses over to those already counted is to give a half-shekel, according to the sanctuary shekel — twenty gerahs to the shekel. This half-shekel is a contribution to YHWH. **14** Everyone who crosses over to those counted, from twenty years old and above, is to give YHWH's contribution. **15** The wealthy are not to give more than a half-shekel, and the poor are not to give less, when you give YHWH's contribution to make atonement for your lives. **16** Take the atonement money from the Sons of Israel and use it for the service of the tent of meeting. It will stand as a memorial before YHWH on behalf of the Sons of Israel, to make atonement for your lives."

---

### The Bronze Basin — Washing Before You Serve (vv. 17–21)

**17** Then YHWH spoke to Moses: **18** "Make a bronze basin with a bronze stand for washing. Place it between the tent of meeting and the altar, and put water in it. **19** Aaron and his sons are to wash their hands and feet from it. **20** Whenever they enter the tent of meeting, they must wash with water so they will not die. Whenever they approach the altar to serve, to burn a fire offering to YHWH, **21** they must wash their hands and feet so they will not die. This is to be a lasting ordinance for Aaron and his descendants throughout their generations."

---

### The Sacred Anointing Oil — A Formula Reserved for God (vv. 22–33)

**22** Then YHWH spoke to Moses: **23** "Take the finest spices: five hundred shekels of flowing myrrh, half as much — two hundred and fifty shekels — of fragrant cinnamon, two hundred and fifty shekels of fragrant cane, **24** five hundred shekels of cassia — all measured by the sanctuary shekel — and a hin of olive oil. **25** Make these into a sacred anointing oil, a perfumer's blend. It will be a sacred anointing oil.

**26** "Anoint the tent of meeting with it, and the ark of the covenant law, **27** and the table with all its utensils, and the lampstand with its utensils, and the altar of incense, **28** and the altar of burnt offering with all its utensils, and the basin with its stand. **29** Consecrate them so they will be most holy. Whatever touches them will become holy.

**30** "Anoint Aaron and his sons and consecrate them to serve me as priests. **31** Say to the Sons of Israel: 'This is to be my sacred anointing oil throughout your generations. **32** It must not be poured on the body of any ordinary person, and you must not make anything with the same formula. It is holy, and you are to regard it as holy. **33** Whoever blends perfume like it, or whoever puts it on an outsider, that person is to be cut off from his people.'"

---

### The Sacred Incense — A Fragrance Belonging to God Alone (vv. 34–38)

**34** Then YHWH said to Moses: "Take fragrant spices — stacte, onycha, and galbanum — these fragrant spices along with pure frankincense, each in equal amounts, **35** and make a blend of incense, the work of a perfumer, seasoned with salt, pure and holy. **36** Grind some of it to fine powder and place it before the covenant law in the tent of meeting, where I will meet with you. It is to be most holy to you. **37** The incense you make with this formula — you must not make any for yourselves. You are to regard it as holy, reserved for YHWH. **38** Whoever makes incense like it to enjoy its fragrance is to be cut off from his people."

---

## Meaning Notes

**vv. 1–10 — THE ALTAR OF INCENSE: FRAGRANCE AS PRAYER (Heb. *ve'asita mizbeach miqtar qetoret atsei shittim ta'aseh oto*, וְעָשִׂ֥יתָ מִזְבֵּ֖חַ מִקְטַ֣ר קְטֹ֑רֶת עֲצֵ֥י שִׁטִּ֖ים תַּעֲשֶׂ֥ה אֹתֽוֹ):** The phrase *mizbeach miqtar qetoret* (מִזְבֵּחַ מִקְטַר קְטֹרֶת) is literally "an altar of the burning of incense." The root *q-t-r* (קטר) means "to send up in smoke, to cause to rise as vapor," and it appears three times in different forms in this opening phrase — an emphatic concentration of the idea of rising fragrance. The *qetoret* (קְטֹרֶת, "incense") is not merely a pleasant smell; it is a ritual action that produces a visible, rising cloud. This rising smoke became one of the most enduring metaphors for prayer in the biblical tradition (Ps. 141:2, "Let my prayer be set before you like incense"; Rev. 5:8, "golden bowls full of incense, which are the prayers of the saints"). The altar is small — one cubit by one cubit, two cubits high (approximately 1.5 feet square and 3 feet tall) — far smaller than the bronze altar of burnt offering. It is overlaid with pure gold (*zahav tahor*, זָהָ֣ב טָה֔וֹר), marking it as an object of the highest sanctity. Its placement is critical: *lifnei happarokhet asher al-aron ha'edut lifnei hakkapporet asher al-ha'edut asher ivva'ed lekha shammah* (לִפְנֵ֣י הַפָּרֹ֗כֶת אֲשֶׁ֤ר עַל־אֲרֹן֙ הָעֵדֻ֔ת לִפְנֵ֥י הַכַּפֹּ֖רֶת אֲשֶׁ֣ר עַל־הָעֵדֻ֑ת אֲשֶׁ֛ר אִוָּעֵ֥ד לְךָ֖ שָֽׁמָּה, "before the curtain that is over the ark of the testimony, before the atonement cover that is over the testimony, where I will meet with you there"). The incense altar stands as close to the divine presence as one can get without passing through the curtain. It is the last object before the Holy of Holies — the frontier of human approach. The LXX renders *miqtar qetoret* as *thysiastērion thymiamatos* (θυσιαστήριον θυμιάματος, "altar of incense"), using *thymiama* from *thyō* ("to burn, to sacrifice"), preserving the sacrificial dimension of incense.

The instruction that Aaron burn incense *babboqer babboqer* (בַּבֹּ֣קֶר בַּבֹּ֔קֶר, "morning by morning" — the doubling intensifying regularity) when he tends (*hetiV*, הֵיטִיב, literally "makes good") the lamps creates a pairing of light and fragrance: every morning the priest trims the lampstand and fills the sanctuary with incense. Light and scent together constitute the sensory environment of God's presence. The prohibition against *qetoret zarah* (קְטֹ֣רֶת זָרָ֔ה, "strange/unauthorized incense") — and against any burnt offering, grain offering, or drink offering on this altar — marks it as exclusively dedicated to the single act of incense. It has one purpose and no other.

The annual atonement on the horns (*vekhipper Aharon al-qarnotav achat bashshanah*, וְכִפֶּ֤ר אַהֲרֹן֙ עַל־קַרְנֹתָ֔יו אַחַ֥ת בַּשָּׁנָ֖ה, "and Aaron shall make atonement on its horns once a year") refers to Yom Kippur — the Day of Atonement. Even the altar of incense, despite being gold and pure and positioned nearest to God, requires annual purification. Nothing in the created order — not even the holiest object — stays clean by itself. The verse concludes with the declaration *qodesh-qodashim hi' laYHWH* (קֹֽדֶשׁ־קׇדָשִׁ֥ים הִ֖וא לַיהוָֽה, "it is most holy to YHWH"), the superlative of sanctity.

**vv. 11–16 — THE HALF-SHEKEL: RANSOM AND EQUALITY (Heb. *ki tissa et-rosh benei-yisra'el lifqudeihem venatenu ish kofer nafsho laYHWH bifqod otam*, כִּ֣י תִשָּׂ֞א אֶת־רֹ֥אשׁ בְּנֵֽי־יִשְׂרָאֵל֮ לִפְקֻדֵיהֶם֒ וְנָ֨תְנ֜וּ אִ֣ישׁ כֹּ֧פֶר נַפְשׁ֛וֹ לַיהוָ֖ה בִּפְקֹ֣ד אֹתָ֑ם):** The opening phrase *ki tissa et-rosh* (כִּי תִשָּׂא אֶת־רֹאשׁ, "when you lift up the head") is the Hebrew idiom for taking a census — literally "lifting the head" of each person to count them. This phrase gives the book of Numbers its Hebrew name (*Bemidbar* notwithstanding, the census itself is introduced with this very phrase in Num. 1:2). But counting is dangerous: *velo-yihyeh vahem negef bifqod otam* (וְלֹא־יִהְיֶ֨ה בָהֶ֥ם נֶ֛גֶף בִּפְקֹ֥ד אֹתָ֖ם, "so there will not be a plague among them when they are counted"). To number people is to assert power over them; it is the act of a king or a conqueror. When God numbers Israel, the danger is that numbering reduces persons to units. The *kofer* (כֹּפֶר, "ransom, covering price") — from the same root *k-p-r* as *kippur* (atonement) — is what each individual pays to buy back his life from the danger of being counted.

The amount is *machatsit hasheqel* (מַחֲצִ֣ית הַשֶּׁ֗קֶל, "half of the shekel"), specified as *besheqel haqqodesh* (בְּשֶׁ֣קֶל הַקֹּ֔דֶשׁ, "by the sanctuary shekel") of twenty gerahs. The half-shekel is remarkably democratic: *he'ashir lo-yarbeh vehadal lo yam'it* (הֶעָשִׁ֣יר לֹֽא־יַרְבֶּ֗ה וְהַדַּל֙ לֹ֣א יַמְעִ֔יט, "the rich shall not give more and the poor shall not give less"). Before God, every life has exactly the same value. No one can buy greater standing; no one is discounted. The LXX uses *lytra* (λύτρα, "ransom money") for *kofer*, the same root that appears in *lytron* (λύτρον, "ransom") in Mark 10:45, where Jesus says the Son of Man came "to give his life as a ransom for many." The half-shekel is designated *kesef hakkippurim* (כֶּ֣סֶף הַכִּפֻּרִ֗ים, "the money of atonements") and serves as a *zikkaron* (זִכָּרוֹן, "memorial, reminder") before YHWH — the silver itself testifies on Israel's behalf that their lives have been ransomed.

**vv. 17–21 — THE BRONZE BASIN: THE NECESSITY OF WASHING (Heb. *ve'asita kiyyor nechoshet vekanno nechoshet lerachtsah*, וְעָשִׂ֜יתָ כִּיּ֥וֹר נְחֹ֛שֶׁת וְכַנּ֥וֹ נְחֹ֖שֶׁת לְרׇחְצָ֑ה):** The *kiyyor* (כִּיּוֹר, "basin, laver") is introduced with remarkable brevity — no dimensions are given, unlike the detailed measurements for every other tabernacle object. What matters is its function: *lerachtsah* (לְרׇחְצָה, "for washing"). Its placement *bein-ohel mo'ed uvein hammizbeach* (בֵּין־אֹ֤הֶל מוֹעֵד֙ וּבֵ֣ין הַמִּזְבֵּ֔חַ, "between the tent of meeting and the altar") puts it at the threshold of service — the priest must pass through water before he can approach either the altar or the holy place. The consequence for neglecting this washing is death: *velo yamuthu* (וְלֹ֣א יָמֻ֔תוּ, "so that they will not die"), repeated twice (vv. 20, 21) for emphasis. The washing of hands and feet (*verachatsu yedeihem veragleihem*, וְרָחֲצ֛וּ יְדֵיהֶ֥ם וְרַגְלֵיהֶ֖ם) maps onto the same extremities marked with blood in 29:20 — the hand that serves and the foot that walks. What was consecrated with blood must be continuously maintained with water. The LXX adds *niptēr* (νιπτήρα, "wash basin"), and the washing verb *niptō* (νίπτω) is the same verb used when Jesus washes the disciples' feet in John 13:5.

**vv. 22–33 — THE SACRED ANOINTING OIL: A RECIPE THAT CANNOT BE REPLICATED (Heb. *ve'attah qach-lekha besamim rosh mor-deror chamesh me'ot*, וְאַתָּ֣ה קַח־לְךָ֮ בְּשָׂמִ֣ים רֹאשׁ֒ מׇר־דְּר֞וֹר חֲמֵ֣שׁ מֵא֗וֹת):** The phrase *besamim rosh* (בְּשָׂמִים רֹאשׁ, "chief/head spices, finest spices") indicates the highest quality. The four spices — *mor-deror* (מׇר־דְּרוֹר, "flowing myrrh," literally "myrrh of freedom" — the best quality myrrh that flows freely without being pressed), *qinnemon-besem* (קִנְּמׇן־בֶּשֶׂם, "fragrant cinnamon"), *qeneh-vosem* (קְנֵה־בֹשֶׂם, "fragrant cane/calamus"), and *qiddah* (קִדָּה, "cassia") — are combined with a *hin* of olive oil (*shemen zayit*, שֶׁמֶן זַיִת). The mixture is made as a *roqeach mirqachat* (רֹקֵ֔חַ מִרְקַ֖חַת, "a perfumer's compound"), using the professional vocabulary of the ancient spice-blender. The word *roqeach* (רֹקֵחַ) is from the root *r-q-ch* meaning "to mix spices, to compound perfume" — this is artisanal work.

The oil anoints everything in the tabernacle: the tent, the ark, the table, the lampstand, both altars, the basin. Then it anoints Aaron and his sons. The sequence is significant: objects first, then persons. The entire sacred environment is anointed before the priests are. The result: *veqiddashta otam vehavu qodesh qodashim kol-hannogea' bahem yiqdash* (וְקִדַּשְׁתָּ֣ אֹתָ֔ם וְהָי֖וּ קֹ֣דֶשׁ קׇדָשִׁ֑ים כׇּל־הַנֹּגֵ֥עַ בָּהֶ֖ם יִקְדָּֽשׁ, "and you shall consecrate them and they shall be most holy; whatever touches them shall become holy"). Holiness is again contagious — it spreads by contact.

The prohibition against replication is absolute: *al-besar adam lo yisakh* (עַל־בְּשַׂ֤ר אָדָם֙ לֹ֣א יִיסָ֔ךְ, "on the flesh of a human being it shall not be poured") and *uvematkuneto lo ta'asu kamohu* (וּבְמַ֨תְכֻּנְתּ֔וֹ לֹ֥א תַעֲשׂ֖וּ כָּמֹ֑הוּ, "and in its proportion/formula you shall not make anything like it"). The penalty is *nikhrat* (נִכְרַת, "cut off") — excision from the community. The LXX uses *exolethreuthēsetai* (ἐξολεθρευθήσεται, "shall be utterly destroyed"), intensifying the Hebrew. The anointing oil is not a product but a person's identity: it belongs to God, and to reproduce it is to claim what belongs exclusively to the divine.

**vv. 34–38 — THE SACRED INCENSE: A FRAGRANCE FOR GOD ALONE (Heb. *qach-lekha sammim nataf ushechelet vechelbenah sammim ulevonah zakkah*, קַח־לְךָ֣ סַמִּ֗ים נָטָ֤ף וּשְׁחֵ֙לֶת֙ וְחֶלְבְּנָ֔ה סַמִּ֖ים וּלְבֹנָ֣ה זַכָּ֑ה):** Four fragrant substances compose the holy incense: *nataf* (נָטָף, "stacte" — a resin that "drops" or "drips," from *nataf*, "to drip"), *shechelet* (שְׁחֵלֶת, "onycha" — likely the aromatic operculum of a sea mollusk), *chelbenah* (חֶלְבְּנָה, "galbanum" — a pungent gum resin), and *levonah zakkah* (לְבֹנָה זַכָּה, "pure frankincense"). The mixture is made *bad bevad* (בַּ֥ד בְּבַ֖ד, "part for part, equal amounts") — each ingredient in the same proportion, no one dominating the others. The blend is described as *roqach ma'aseh roqeach* (רֹ֔קַח מַעֲשֵׂ֥ה רוֹקֵ֖חַ, "a compound, the work of a perfumer"), *memullach* (מְמֻלָּ֖ח, "salted, seasoned with salt" — salt as a preservative and a sign of covenant, cf. Lev. 2:13), *tahor* (טָה֥וֹר, "pure"), and *qodesh* (קֹֽדֶשׁ, "holy").

A portion is to be ground fine (*veshachaqta mimmennah hadeq*, וְשָֽׁחַקְתָּ֣ מִמֶּ֗נָּה הָדֵק֒, "and you shall grind some of it fine") and placed *lifnei ha'edut be'ohel mo'ed* (לִפְנֵ֤י הָעֵדֻת֙ בְּאֹ֣הֶל מוֹעֵ֔ד, "before the testimony in the tent of meeting"). The finest powder of the sacred incense goes to the closest place to God's presence. As with the oil, the prohibition against personal use is total: *lo ta'asu lakhem bematkunetah* (לֹ֥א תַעֲשׂ֖וּ לָכֶ֑ם בְּמַ֨תְכֻּנְתָּ֔הּ, "you shall not make for yourselves in its formula"). The punishment is the same: *nikhrat me'ammav* (נִכְרַ֖ת מֵעַמָּֽיו, "cut off from his people"). The incense exists for one purpose — to rise before YHWH — and cannot be domesticated for human enjoyment. The LXX again uses *exolethreuthēsetai* (ἐξολεθρευθήσεται) for the penalty of *karet*.

---

## Translator's Reflection

**Smoke, silver, water, and spice — the four elements of sacred proximity:**

Exodus 30 is a chapter of thresholds. After the dramatic ordination of Exodus 29 — with its blood and seven days and the staggering promise "I will dwell among them" — chapter 30 turns to the quieter but equally essential infrastructure of ongoing proximity. If chapter 29 asked how a priest is made, chapter 30 asks how a priest keeps approaching. The answer involves four things: fragrance, ransom, water, and recipe.

**1. The incense altar is the smallest altar with the largest meaning.** It measures one cubit by one cubit — barely eighteen inches square — and yet it is overlaid with pure gold and positioned closer to the ark than any other object in the tabernacle. The bronze altar of burnt offering, with its dramatic animal sacrifices and splashing blood, stands outside in the courtyard. The golden incense altar stands inside, just before the curtain, in the quiet, lamplit space where the only offering is smoke. The contrast is deliberate. The outer altar deals with the cost of sin — blood, flesh, fire. The inner altar deals with the substance of communion — fragrance rising, prayer ascending, a sweet-smelling cloud filling the space between God and the priest. Every morning and every evening, Aaron tends the lamps and burns incense. Light and scent, sight and smell — these are the sensory markers of the place where God meets humanity. The incense altar is not dramatic. It is intimate. And intimacy, in this chapter, is more demanding than drama.

**2. The half-shekel is the most radically egalitarian moment in the Torah.** "The rich shall not give more and the poor shall not give less." In a world where wealth determined worth — where kings built temples proportional to their treasuries and the powerful purchased favor — YHWH insists on a flat rate. Half a shekel. The same from every person. No one's life costs more than another's. No one's ransom is discounted. The rich man's silver and the poor man's silver weigh exactly the same on God's scales. This is not merely an administrative convenience; it is a theological declaration. Every human life has the same value before YHWH — not because humans are all the same, but because what makes a life valuable is not what the person possesses but the fact that God claims it. The census is dangerous precisely because numbering people tempts the counter to rank them. The half-shekel prevents ranking. It forces equality at the moment when inequality is most tempting.

The word *kofer* — ransom — is profoundly suggestive. Each person is, in a sense, purchasing their own life back. From whom? Not from a kidnapper, but from the danger inherent in being counted, being known, being visible to divine assessment. To be numbered is to be examined. The ransom covers the person at the moment of exposure. Later tradition connected this to the annual temple tax (cf. Matt. 17:24-27, where Jesus discusses the two-drachma tax and instructs Peter to find a coin in a fish's mouth). The half-shekel endured as a concrete expression of the principle that every Israelite belongs to God and that belonging has a cost — but a cost that is identical for everyone.

**3. The basin has no measurements, only a mandate: wash or die.** Every other tabernacle object comes with precise dimensions. The bronze altar: five cubits by five cubits. The ark: two and a half cubits long. The table: two cubits by one cubit. The incense altar: one cubit by one cubit. But the basin — the *kiyyor* — is described only by its material (bronze), its position (between the tent and the altar), and its function (washing). No length, no width, no depth. The silence about measurements is itself eloquent. What matters about the basin is not its size but its use. It stands at the intersection of every priestly movement: you cannot go from the courtyard to the tent without passing it. You cannot approach the altar without it being there, waiting, full of water. The command is stark: wash your hands and feet, or die. Not "wash or be ritually impure." Not "wash or be temporarily excluded." Wash or die. The stakes of approaching God with unwashed hands are lethal. This is not about hygiene. It is about the continuous recognition that the priest, even after being consecrated with blood and oil and seven days of ritual, must be cleansed again every single time he approaches. Ordination is not a permanent state of readiness. It is a threshold that must be crossed again and again, daily, through water.

**4. The sacred recipes are the most surprising prohibition in the chapter.** God gives Israel two formulas — one for anointing oil, one for incense — and immediately forbids anyone from reproducing them for personal use. The ingredients are not secret; they are listed in detail. The proportions are not hidden; they are specified exactly. And yet: "You must not make anything with the same formula." The prohibition is not about secrecy but about ownership. The fragrance belongs to God. The recipe is published, but the product is reserved. This is a distinction modern culture struggles to understand: knowing how something is made does not give you the right to make it. The anointing oil and the incense exist for a single purpose — to mark and fill the space where God is present — and any attempt to domesticate them, to enjoy them privately, to treat the holy as a consumer product, results in the most severe penalty the community can inflict: being cut off from your people.

The galbanum in the incense is particularly interesting. Unlike the other ingredients, galbanum has a sharp, almost unpleasant smell on its own. The Talmud (Keritot 6b) draws from this the principle that every community requires the participation of its less agreeable members: even the sinners of Israel must be included in the community's prayer, just as galbanum is included in the incense. The sacred fragrance is not composed only of pleasant elements. It includes the pungent, the sharp, the difficult — and the combination, in equal parts, produces something holy that none of the ingredients could produce alone.

**5. The chapter's four sections trace a complete theology of approach.** Incense (prayer), ransom (atonement), water (purification), and sacred recipe (holiness that cannot be replicated or commercialized) — these are the four requirements for ongoing life in God's presence. You need fragrance — something that rises, that carries human devotion upward. You need ransom — the acknowledgment that standing before God costs something and that the cost is the same for everyone. You need water — the continuous cleansing that makes yesterday's consecration effective today. And you need to respect the boundary between the sacred and the common — to resist the impulse to mass-produce holiness, to privatize what belongs to God, to turn the instruments of worship into lifestyle products. Exodus 30 is the chapter that insists the infrastructure of divine presence is not a one-time construction project but an ongoing maintenance regimen. The tabernacle is built. The priests are ordained. Now: how do you sustain it? With smoke and silver and water and spice, day after day, morning and evening, equal shares from rich and poor alike, hands and feet washed clean every time you approach, and the sacred scent rising — always rising — into the space where God meets the ones who come near.

— *PNEUMA*
